title: Baloney Detection Kit
topic: Critical thinking — Carl Sagan's Baloney Detection Kit, adapted for 3rd grade as 8 Detective Tools

bee_quiz.question: Saying "How do you know that?" before believing a claim is using which detective tool?
The fancy name for attacking the person instead of their idea is what?
Information passed along like "my friend told me so" — without any real proof — is called what?
The Baloney Detective Agency teaches how many detective tools in total?
Two things happening together but NOT actually causing each other is the trap called what?
Showing only the evidence that supports your side and hiding the rest is called what?
A boy's lucky socks were worn on three days his team won. Which conclusion is most correct?

choices.a: The socks definitely caused the wins
choices.b: Wearing the socks more often will keep the team winning
choices.c: The wins and the socks happened together but the socks did not cause the wins
choices.d: The socks have a 75% chance of causing wins

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Students often assume that two events happening together prove one caused the other (Tool #3: Coincidence Trap). The lucky-socks example exists in the kit specifically to break this misconception.

bee_quiz.question: A classmate says "Don't trust her science project, she has messy hair." This is wrong because:

choices.a: Hair has nothing to do with whether the science is correct
choices.b: Messy hair usually means smarter people
choices.c: Only neat people can do good science
choices.d: Science projects don't need to be correct

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Students may judge ideas based on the appearance or personality of the person making them, instead of the evidence (Tool #6: Ad Hominem).

bee_quiz.question: An advert says "9 out of 10 dentists recommend BrightSmile!" — but they tested 200 dentists. The smartest question to ask is:

choices.b: What did the other 190 dentists say?

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Students often miss that '9 out of 10' could be cherry-picked from a much larger sample where most disagreed (Tool #4: Don't Cherry-Pick).

bee_quiz.question: Which of these is the STRONGEST evidence that a claim is true?

choices.a: Your best friend told you so
choices.b: An advert says it on TV
choices.c: Many independent scientists tested it and got the same result
choices.d: Lots of people on the internet agree

bee_quiz.question: A pea-sized battery claims to last 1,000 hours, but normal ones last 10. Which detective tool spots this baloney?

choices.c: Tool #8: Do the Numbers Add Up?

bee_quiz.question: "Brushing teeth every night → fewer cavities" — this relationship is an example of which type?
Which of the following is NOT one of the eight Detective Tools in the kit?
All of the following count as REAL EVIDENCE for a claim EXCEPT:

choices.a: Physical proof you can hold and measure
choices.c: Tests done independently by different scientists

bee_quiz.question: Which of these is NOT a baloney threat tactic?

choices.a: "Share this or BAD THINGS will happen to you!"
choices.b: "Believe my cream OR your skin will keep getting worse!"
choices.c: "If you don't repost, you'll have bad luck for 7 years!"
choices.d: "I tested it on 100 people and 70 got better."

word_cards.definition: Something that sounds true but isn't. Often used to fool people.
word_cards.example: "My lucky socks made my team win!" — that's baloney.

word_cards.definition: Facts or proof you can actually check — like measurements, photos, or tests. NOT just someone's word.
word_cards.example: Real evidence: scales, claws, or bones from a creature, not just "a friend told me."

word_cards.definition: Information passed from one person to another without any real proof.
word_cards.example: "My cousin's friend saw a UFO" — that's hearsay, not evidence.

word_cards.definition: Two things happening together just by chance, with no real link between them.
word_cards.example: Wearing red socks on a day your team wins — they happened together but the socks didn't cause the win.

word_cards.definition: Showing only the facts that help your side and hiding the ones that don't.
word_cards.example: An advert that says "9 of 10 dentists agree!" but quietly skips the 190 who didn't.

word_cards.definition: Attacking the person instead of their idea. The fancy Latin name for it.
word_cards.example: "Don't listen to her science — she has messy hair." Hair has nothing to do with the facts.

word_cards.definition: When one thing actually makes another thing happen, with a clear reason you can show.
word_cards.example: Brushing teeth every night → fewer cavities. Germs → sickness.

word_cards.definition: When different people or scientists check the same thing on their own. If they all get the same answer, the proof is much stronger.
word_cards.example: Three labs in three different countries each test a medicine and all find the same result.

word_cards.definition: A thinking rule that says you can't decide something is true for everyone based on just a few examples.
word_cards.example: Three sour strawberries from one shop don't prove every strawberry in the world is sour.

word_cards.definition: When someone says "bad things will happen if you don't believe me" or "good things if you do." That's a fear-or-bribery trick — not real proof.
word_cards.example: "Share this with 20 friends or BAD THINGS will happen!" Real claims don't need threats.

self_check.half_right_distractors: Q5 (Coincidence Trap variants), Q11 (Tool #N variants), Q15 (statistic-style distractor) = 3
self_check.instrument_or_unit: BDK has no measurement content; rule does not apply
self_check.buzzer_friendly_rear_loaded: Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q5, Q6, Q12 = 7 of 12 non-negative-selection (≥ half)
